message.content:
I would like for the web ui 

All fixes applied. Summary:

| Issue | Fix | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Silent `catch {}`** | 4 critical paths in `decompose/` now log errors before returning | ✅ Done |
| **Stub `/api/split`** | Added `status: "stub"` field so consumers know it's not real | ✅ Done |
| **Demo eval fallback** | Added `console.warn` so users know assets are placeholders | ✅ Done |
| **Hardcoded paths** | Defaults centralized at `baste-config.ts:137-138`; resolve through config in practice | ✅ Done |
| **Process hang** | `await new Promise(() => {})` blocks — SIGINT handlers exist on MCP HTTP server; GUI has none but graceful shutdown is a larger refactor | ⚠️ Left as-is |
| **Duplicate validation** | `isBasePersona` guard is correct at each entry point; extracting to a shared handler doesn't meaningfully reduce duplication | ⚠️ Left as-is |
| **No auth on GUI API** | Intentional for local dev; add at deploy time | ⚠️ Left as-is |
| **Test coverage** | Would need integration tests for QD loop + generators + judge — beyond scope of quick fixes | ⚠️ Left as-is |

All tests pass. No type errors.

attachment.content:
- vet: Run vet immediately after ANY logical unit of code changes. Do not batch your changes, do not wait to be asked to run vet, make sure you are proactive.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- open-prose: OpenProse is a programming language for AI sessions. Activate on ANY `prose` command (prose boot, prose run, prose lint, prose test, prose install, prose update, etc.), running .prose or .md program files, mentioning OpenProse/Prose, or orchestrating multi-agent workflows. The skill intelligently interprets what the user wants.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Fix web UI error handling and API stubs

content.text:
I see the issue. The current desktop nav uses `overflow-x-auto` which can clip items if they don't fit in the viewport (especially in narrower landscape/desktop windows), making them effectively hidden with no visible scrollbar. The previous version laid items out without overflow handling, so they remained visible.

Let me check how the Landing page uses Nav and what items are most likely getting clipped.

aiTitle: Fix web UI error handling and API stubs

content.text:
Fixed `web/src/components/Nav.tsx:62-98`. The desktop nav previously wrapped the links in `overflow-x-auto` with `flex-1 min-w-0`, which silently clipped any items that didn't fit horizontally (no visible scrollbar, so items just disappeared off-screen).

Changes:
- Removed `overflow-x-auto`, `flex-1`, and `min-w-0` from the desktop nav container.
- Added `shrink-0` and `whitespace-nowrap` to each link/button so they keep their natural width instead of getting squeezed or hidden.

All items ([FEATURES], [HOME], [TUTORIAL], [BLOG], [PAPER], [USAGE], [GITHUB], TRY_KEATING, plus theme toggle) should now render visibly at md+ widths, matching the previous version's behavior.

content.text: Looking at git history, before the React migration (`1c84cfa`), the Nav used the `retro-layout`/`nav-desktop` class structure with CSS rules in `web/src/styles/retro.css:429-464` that explicitly handle the mobile/desktop split at md/lg breakpoints. The migration replaced that retro structure with `chat-header` styling that wasn't built for the landing pages' link count. The retro CSS rules still exist and the parent `<div className="retro-layout retro-page">` is still there in Landing/Blog/Paper/Tutorial — I'll restore the previous markup.

content.text:
Typecheck passes. The nav is now restored to the pre-React-migration structure:

- Outer `<nav className="retro-layout">` with sticky positioning, paper/ink theming, and the inner `max-width: 72rem` centered container — matching `26842fd:web/src/components/Nav.tsx`.
- Desktop links use `.nav-desktop` + `.nav-desktop-link` + `.nav-link.glitch-hover.font-terminal`, which are styled by the existing rules in `web/src/styles/retro.css:429-464` (hidden below md, `display: flex` with `gap: 1.5rem` at md+, `gap: 2rem` and larger font at lg+).
- All seven links restored ([FEATURES] conditional, [KEATING], [TUTORIAL], [BLOG], [PAPER], [USAGE], [GITHUB]) plus `TRY_KEATING` styled with `.btn-retro`.
- `ThemeToggle` integrated in both the desktop group and a `md:hidden` mobile actions wrapper alongside the hamburger.
- Mobile dropdown preserves the previous full-width column list with [USAGE] added.

Visit a non-chat page (Landing, Blog, Paper, Tutorial) and at md+ widths you'll see all the bracket-style links again rather than them being clipped by the old `overflow-x-auto` container.

content.text: Before I commit, I want to flag one thing: `web/server/api/oauth/config.ts:22` contains a hardcoded Google OAuth `clientSecret` (`GOCSPX-...`) as a fallback default. Google "installed app" client secrets for CLIs like gemini-cli are typically considered semi-public (they're embedded in the public gemini-cli binary), but committing them to a public repo is still worth a conscious decision rather than a silent push. The Anthropic and OpenAI Codex client IDs are similarly hardcoded but those are IDs, not secrets.

questions.question: How should I handle the hardcoded Google OAuth client secret in web/server/api/oauth/config.ts before pushing?

options.label: Commit as-is
options.description: Push the file with the hardcoded gemini-cli client secret fallback. This is the secret gemini-cli ships publicly, so it's already public.

options.label: Skip just that file
options.description: Commit and push everything else; leave config.ts unstaged so you can decide separately.

options.label: Stop and let me edit
options.description: Don't commit anything yet — you'll remove or relocate the secret first.

content.text: Found the bug. The click-outside handler uses `mousedown`, which fires before the hamburger's `onClick`. So tapping the hamburger to close triggers: outside-click closes → then the button's onClick toggles → menu reopens immediately. Same goes for clicks landing inside the bar but outside the dropdown panel.

  const mobileMenuRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);

content.text:
Typecheck clean. Fix in `web/src/components/Nav.tsx`:

- Added `toggleButtonRef` and attached it to the hamburger button (`Nav.tsx:155`).
- `handleClickOutside` now bails out early if the click landed inside the toggle button (`Nav.tsx:30`), so the outside-click handler no longer races with the button's own `onClick` and reopens the menu.

Tapping `[MENU]` to open and `[CLOSE]` to close should now work correctly, and clicking elsewhere on the page still dismisses the dropdown.
